Pinchot's declaration brings the
issue square into the open and 1t
was received with cheers by the
delegates to the congress. Again
and again they cheered the man
who stands, above all others, as
the exponent of the Roosevelt ideas
in the present administration. Ev-
eryone is wondering here what
Ballinger will say when he takes
the rostrum tomorrow afternoon
and how he will answer questions
that will be put to him by dele
gates who want to know how he
will explain his attitude.


Pinchot's address was a clear ex-
position of the Roosevelt policies
of protection to the common people
of the country and declaration
of unmistakable war against the
waterpower trust and the admins
tration of law by technicalities.


Give People tho Benefit


Rigid construction Of the law
works and must work." he said, lin
the vast majority Of cases for the
benefit OF the men who can hire
the best lawyers and who have the
sources of influence In law making
at their command. Strict construc-
ton necessarily favors the great
interests as against the people.
Wise execution Of the law must
consider what the law ought to
accomplish for the general good.
The great oppressive trusts ex-
list because of subservient law
makers and adroit legal construc-
tons.


Here is the central stronghold
of the money power in the ever
lasting conflict of the few to grab
and the many to keep or win the
rights they were born with. Legal
technicalities help some people.
The people. not the law. should
have the benefit of every doubt"


Pinchot's unmistakable refer
ence to the various decisions of
Secretary Ballinger that his pre-
decessor had not followed the law
In administering the reclamation
act, set the delegates aghast as
they listened to these plain words.


There could be no better Illus
tration OF the eager, rapid. un.
wearied absorption by capital of
the rights which belong to all the
people," declared Pinchot, than
the waterpower trust, not yet form
ed but In rapid process Of forma-
ton. This statement Is true but
not unchallenged. We are met at
every turn by the indignant denial
OF the waterpower interests.
